{{Short description|Archaeological site in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Motonobaru Site | native_name =本野原遺跡 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = | map_type = Japan Miyazaki Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Motonobaru Site | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|31|49|23|N|131|16|57|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Miyazaki, Miyazaki]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Jōmon period]] | cultures = | discovered = 2000 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = None | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|49|23|N|131|16|57|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Motonobaru Site'''|本野原遺跡| Motonobaru iseki}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of a complex settlement trace primarily from the [[Jōmon period]], located in the Tano neighborhood of the city of [[Miyazaki (city)|Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]] on the of [[Kyushu]] [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164591|title=Database of National Cultural Properties |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref> More than 100 [[pit dwelling]]s have been excavated, and along with the [[Yokomine Site]] on [[Tanegashima]], it is the largest ring settlement to have been found in western Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Overview== Motonobaru Site is located on a plateau at an elevation of approximately 180 meters on an [[alluvial fan]] at the foot of Mt. Wanitsukayama. In 2000, the Tano Town Board of Education conducted an [[archaeological excavation]] in conjunction with a prefectural farmland conservation and improvement project, and found that it was a complex of ruins of a large-scale village that began in the [[Japanese Paleolithic]] period and continued into the Jōmon period. This is extremely rare in the Kyushu region or indeed anywhere in western Japan west of [[Aichi Prefecture]]. The village was constructed in a ring shape of about 100 meters in diameter with earthen storage pits, dug pillar buildings, and [[pit dwelling]]s centered around a central plaza. The pit dwellings are concentrated on the north and east sides of the ring, and earthen pits are distributed on the outside adjacent to the plaza. There is considerable overlap on the foundations of these structures, indicating that they were rebuilt over centuries of occupation. Some of them were found to contain carbonized nuts, where as other contained bone powder, and thus may have been graves. The plaza itself is thought to be a ritual space, wit a stone arrangement and earthen pits at the center.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
On the north side of the ring, dug-out pillared buildings are lined up in an orderly north–south direction. Eleven buildings with dug-out pillars were found in a row and 10 in a ring, but the pillar holes in three of the rows were extremely large, 80 cm in diameter, which is similar to those in eastern Japan. In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
A large amount of [[Jōmon pottery]] was excavated from the southeastern slope, totaling 179,237 pieces with a total weight of 2.8 tons. Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the [[stone tool]]s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of [[maize weevil]]s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, [[cockroach]] egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from [[Tano Station (Miyazaki)|Tano Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.miyazaki-archive.jp/d-museum/mch/details/view/1838 Miyazaki Cultural Heritage Information] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.miyazaki.miyazaki.jp/faq/doc/1316.html Miyazaki city home page]{{in lang|ja}}

In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
A large amount of [[Jōmon pottery]] was excavated from the southeastern slope, totaling 179,237 pieces with a total weight of 2.8 tons. Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the [[stone tool]]s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of [[maize weevil]]s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, [[cockroach]] egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from [[Tano Station (Miyazaki)|Tano Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Motonobaru Site'''|本野原遺跡| Motonobaru iseki}} is an [[archaeological site]] consisting of a complex settlement trace primarily from the [[Jōmon period]], located in the Tano neighborhood of the city of [[Miyazaki (city)|Miyazaki]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]] on the of [[Kyushu]] [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164591|title=Database of National Cultural Properties |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref> More than 100 [[pit dwelling]]s have been excavated, and along with the [[Yokomine Site]] on [[Tanegashima]], it is the largest ring settlement to have been found in western Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Overview== Motonobaru Site is located on a plateau at an elevation of approximately 180 meters on an [[alluvial fan]] at the foot of Mt. Wanitsukayama. In 2000, the Tano Town Board of Education conducted an [[archaeological excavation]] in conjunction with a prefectural farmland conservation and improvement project, and found that it was a complex of ruins of a large-scale village that began in the [[Japanese Paleolithic]] period and continued into the Jōmon period. This is extremely rare in the Kyushu region or indeed anywhere in western Japan west of [[Aichi Prefecture]]. The village was constructed in a ring shape of about 100 meters in diameter with earthen storage pits, dug pillar buildings, and [[pit dwelling]]s centered around a central plaza. The pit dwellings are concentrated on the north and east sides of the ring, and earthen pits are distributed on the outside adjacent to the plaza. There is considerable overlap on the foundations of these structures, indicating that they were rebuilt over centuries of occupation. Some of them were found to contain carbonized nuts, where as other contained bone powder, and thus may have been graves. The plaza itself is thought to be a ritual space, wit a stone arrangement and earthen pits at the center.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
On the north side of the ring, dug-out pillared buildings are lined up in an orderly north–south direction. Eleven buildings with dug-out pillars were found in a row and 10 in a ring, but the pillar holes in three of the rows were extremely large, 80 cm in diameter, which is similar to those in eastern Japan. In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
A large amount of [[Jōmon pottery]] was excavated from the southeastern slope, totaling 179,237 pieces with a total weight of 2.8 tons. Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the [[stone tool]]s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of [[maize weevil]]s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, [[cockroach]] egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from [[Tano Station (Miyazaki)|Tano Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The village was constructed in a ring shape of about 100 meters in diameter with earthen storage pits, dug pillar buildings, and [[pit dwelling]]s centered around a central plaza. The pit dwellings are concentrated on the north and east sides of the ring, and earthen pits are distributed on the outside adjacent to the plaza. There is considerable overlap on the foundations of these structures, indicating that they were rebuilt over centuries of occupation. Some of them were found to contain carbonized nuts, where as other contained bone powder, and thus may have been graves. The plaza itself is thought to be a ritual space, wit a stone arrangement and earthen pits at the center.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
On the north side of the ring, dug-out pillared buildings are lined up in an orderly north–south direction. Eleven buildings with dug-out pillars were found in a row and 10 in a ring, but the pillar holes in three of the rows were extremely large, 80 cm in diameter, which is similar to those in eastern Japan. In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
A large amount of [[Jōmon pottery]] was excavated from the southeastern slope, totaling 179,237 pieces with a total weight of 2.8 tons. Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the [[stone tool]]s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of [[maize weevil]]s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, [[cockroach]] egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from [[Tano Station (Miyazaki)|Tano Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164591|title=Database of National Cultural Properties |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref> More than 100 [[pit dwelling]]s have been excavated, and along with the [[Yokomine Site]] on [[Tanegashima]], it is the largest ring settlement to have been found in western Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Overview== Motonobaru Site is located on a plateau at an elevation of approximately 180 meters on an [[alluvial fan]] at the foot of Mt. Wanitsukayama. In 2000, the Tano Town Board of Education conducted an [[archaeological excavation]] in conjunction with a prefectural farmland conservation and improvement project, and found that it was a complex of ruins of a large-scale village that began in the [[Japanese Paleolithic]] period and continued into the Jōmon period. This is extremely rare in the Kyushu region or indeed anywhere in western Japan west of [[Aichi Prefecture]]. The village was constructed in a ring shape of about 100 meters in diameter with earthen storage pits, dug pillar buildings, and [[pit dwelling]]s centered around a central plaza. The pit dwellings are concentrated on the north and east sides of the ring, and earthen pits are distributed on the outside adjacent to the plaza. There is considerable overlap on the foundations of these structures, indicating that they were rebuilt over centuries of occupation. Some of them were found to contain carbonized nuts, where as other contained bone powder, and thus may have been graves. The plaza itself is thought to be a ritual space, wit a stone arrangement and earthen pits at the center.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
On the north side of the ring, dug-out pillared buildings are lined up in an orderly north–south direction. Eleven buildings with dug-out pillars were found in a row and 10 in a ring, but the pillar holes in three of the rows were extremely large, 80 cm in diameter, which is similar to those in eastern Japan. In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
A large amount of [[Jōmon pottery]] was excavated from the southeastern slope, totaling 179,237 pieces with a total weight of 2.8 tons. Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the [[stone tool]]s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of [[maize weevil]]s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, [[cockroach]] egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from [[Tano Station (Miyazaki)|Tano Station]] on the [[JR Kyushu]] [[Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)]]

The {{nihongo|'''Motonobaru Site'''|本野原遺跡| Motonobaru iseki}} is an [[:en:Archaeological site|Archaeological site]] consisting of a complex settlement trace primarily from the [[Jōmon period]], located in the Tano neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Miyazaki (city)|Miyazaki]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]] on the of [[Kyushu]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164591|title=Database of National Cultural Properties |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref> More than 100 [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s have been excavated, and along with the [[:en:Yokomine Site|Yokomine Site]] on [[:en:Tanegashima|Tanegashima]], it is the largest ring settlement to have been found in western Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Overview== Motonobaru Site is located on a plateau at an elevation of approximately 180 meters on an [[:en:Alluvial fan|Alluvial fan]] at the foot of Mt. Wanitsukayama. In 2000, the Tano Town Board of Education conducted an [[archaeological excavation]] in conjunction with a prefectural farmland conservation and improvement project, and found that it was a complex of ruins of a large-scale village that began in the [[:en:Japanese Paleolithic|Japanese Paleolithic]] period and continued into the Jōmon period. This is extremely rare in the Kyushu region or indeed anywhere in western Japan west of [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]. The village was constructed in a ring shape of about 100 meters in diameter with earthen storage pits, dug pillar buildings, and [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s centered around a central plaza. The pit dwellings are concentrated on the north and east sides of the ring, and earthen pits are distributed on the outside adjacent to the plaza. There is considerable overlap on the foundations of these structures, indicating that they were rebuilt over centuries of occupation. Some of them were found to contain carbonized nuts, where as other contained bone powder, and thus may have been graves. The plaza itself is thought to be a ritual space, wit a stone arrangement and earthen pits at the center.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
On the north side of the ring, dug-out pillared buildings are lined up in an orderly north–south direction. Eleven buildings with dug-out pillars were found in a row and 10 in a ring, but the pillar holes in three of the rows were extremely large, 80 cm in diameter, which is similar to those in eastern Japan. In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
A large amount of [[:en:Jōmon pottery|Jōmon pottery]] was excavated from the southeastern slope, totaling 179,237 pieces with a total weight of 2.8 tons. Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the [[:en:Stone tool|Stone tool]]s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of [[:en:maize weevil|maize weevil]]s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, [[:en:cockroach|cockroach]] egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from [[:en:Tano Station (Miyazaki)|Tano Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nippō Main Line|Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Motonobaru Site]][[ja:本野原遺跡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q85881084]] Wikidata name: Motonobaru Site [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Archaeological site in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Motonobaru Site | native_name =本野原遺跡 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = | map_type = Japan Miyazaki Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Motonobaru Site | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|31|49|23|N|131|16|57|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Miyazaki, Miyazaki|Miyazaki, Miyazaki]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Jōmon period]] | cultures = | discovered = 2000 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = None | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|49|23|N|131|16|57|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Motonobaru Site'''|本野原遺跡| Motonobaru iseki}} is an [[:en:Archaeological site|Archaeological site]] consisting of a complex settlement trace primarily from the [[Jōmon period]], located in the Tano neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Miyazaki (city)|Miyazaki]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]] on the of [[Kyushu]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164591|title=Database of National Cultural Properties |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref> More than 100 [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s have been excavated, and along with the [[:en:Yokomine Site|Yokomine Site]] on [[:en:Tanegashima|Tanegashima]], it is the largest ring settlement to have been found in western Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Overview== Motonobaru Site is located on a plateau at an elevation of approximately 180 meters on an [[:en:Alluvial fan|Alluvial fan]] at the foot of Mt. Wanitsukayama. In 2000, the Tano Town Board of Education conducted an [[archaeological excavation]] in conjunction with a prefectural farmland conservation and improvement project, and found that it was a complex of ruins of a large-scale village that began in the [[:en:Japanese Paleolithic|Japanese Paleolithic]] period and continued into the Jōmon period. This is extremely rare in the Kyushu region or indeed anywhere in western Japan west of [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]. The village was constructed in a ring shape of about 100 meters in diameter with earthen storage pits, dug pillar buildings, and [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s centered around a central plaza. The pit dwellings are concentrated on the north and east sides of the ring, and earthen pits are distributed on the outside adjacent to the plaza. There is considerable overlap on the foundations of these structures, indicating that they were rebuilt over centuries of occupation. Some of them were found to contain carbonized nuts, where as other contained bone powder, and thus may have been graves. The plaza itself is thought to be a ritual space, wit a stone arrangement and earthen pits at the center.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
On the north side of the ring, dug-out pillared buildings are lined up in an orderly north–south direction. Eleven buildings with dug-out pillars were found in a row and 10 in a ring, but the pillar holes in three of the rows were extremely large, 80 cm in diameter, which is similar to those in eastern Japan. In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
A large amount of [[:en:Jōmon pottery|Jōmon pottery]] was excavated from the southeastern slope, totaling 179,237 pieces with a total weight of 2.8 tons. Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the [[:en:Stone tool|Stone tool]]s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of [[:en:maize weevil|maize weevil]]s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, [[:en:cockroach|cockroach]] egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from [[:en:Tano Station (Miyazaki)|Tano Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nippō Main Line|Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.miyazaki-archive.jp/d-museum/mch/details/view/1838 Miyazaki Cultural Heritage Information] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.miyazaki.miyazaki.jp/faq/doc/1316.html Miyazaki city home page]{{in lang|ja}}

The {{nihongo|'''Motonobaru Site'''|本野原遺跡| Motonobaru iseki}} is an [[:en:Archaeological site|Archaeological site]] consisting of a complex settlement trace primarily from the [[Jōmon period]], located in the Tano neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Miyazaki (city)|Miyazaki]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]] on the of [[Kyushu]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164591|title=Database of National Cultural Properties |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref> More than 100 [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s have been excavated, and along with the [[:en:Yokomine Site|Yokomine Site]] on [[:en:Tanegashima|Tanegashima]], it is the largest ring settlement to have been found in western Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Overview== Motonobaru Site is located on a plateau at an elevation of approximately 180 meters on an [[:en:Alluvial fan|Alluvial fan]] at the foot of Mt. Wanitsukayama. In 2000, the Tano Town Board of Education conducted an [[archaeological excavation]] in conjunction with a prefectural farmland conservation and improvement project, and found that it was a complex of ruins of a large-scale village that began in the [[:en:Japanese Paleolithic|Japanese Paleolithic]] period and continued into the Jōmon period. This is extremely rare in the Kyushu region or indeed anywhere in western Japan west of [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]. The village was constructed in a ring shape of about 100 meters in diameter with earthen storage pits, dug pillar buildings, and [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s centered around a central plaza. The pit dwellings are concentrated on the north and east sides of the ring, and earthen pits are distributed on the outside adjacent to the plaza. There is considerable overlap on the foundations of these structures, indicating that they were rebuilt over centuries of occupation. Some of them were found to contain carbonized nuts, where as other contained bone powder, and thus may have been graves. The plaza itself is thought to be a ritual space, wit a stone arrangement and earthen pits at the center.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
On the north side of the ring, dug-out pillared buildings are lined up in an orderly north–south direction. Eleven buildings with dug-out pillars were found in a row and 10 in a ring, but the pillar holes in three of the rows were extremely large, 80 cm in diameter, which is similar to those in eastern Japan. In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
A large amount of [[:en:Jōmon pottery|Jōmon pottery]] was excavated from the southeastern slope, totaling 179,237 pieces with a total weight of 2.8 tons. Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the [[:en:Stone tool|Stone tool]]s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of [[:en:maize weevil|maize weevil]]s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, [[:en:cockroach|cockroach]] egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from [[:en:Tano Station (Miyazaki)|Tano Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nippō Main Line|Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Motonobaru Site]][[ja:本野原遺跡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Motonobaru Site]][[ja:本野原遺跡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q85881084]] Wikidata name: Motonobaru Site [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Archaeological site in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Motonobaru Site | native_name =本野原遺跡 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = | map_type = Japan Miyazaki Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Motonobaru Site | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|31|49|23|N|131|16|57|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Miyazaki, Miyazaki|Miyazaki, Miyazaki]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Jōmon period]] | cultures = | discovered = 2000 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = None | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|49|23|N|131|16|57|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Motonobaru Site'''|本野原遺跡| Motonobaru iseki}} is an [[:en:Archaeological site|Archaeological site]] consisting of a complex settlement trace primarily from the [[Jōmon period]], located in the Tano neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Miyazaki (city)|Miyazaki]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]] on the of [[Kyushu]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164591|title=Database of National Cultural Properties |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref> More than 100 [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s have been excavated, and along with the [[:en:Yokomine Site|Yokomine Site]] on [[:en:Tanegashima|Tanegashima]], it is the largest ring settlement to have been found in western Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Overview== Motonobaru Site is located on a plateau at an elevation of approximately 180 meters on an [[:en:Alluvial fan|Alluvial fan]] at the foot of Mt. Wanitsukayama. In 2000, the Tano Town Board of Education conducted an [[archaeological excavation]] in conjunction with a prefectural farmland conservation and improvement project, and found that it was a complex of ruins of a large-scale village that began in the [[:en:Japanese Paleolithic|Japanese Paleolithic]] period and continued into the Jōmon period. This is extremely rare in the Kyushu region or indeed anywhere in western Japan west of [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]. The village was constructed in a ring shape of about 100 meters in diameter with earthen storage pits, dug pillar buildings, and [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s centered around a central plaza. The pit dwellings are concentrated on the north and east sides of the ring, and earthen pits are distributed on the outside adjacent to the plaza. There is considerable overlap on the foundations of these structures, indicating that they were rebuilt over centuries of occupation. Some of them were found to contain carbonized nuts, where as other contained bone powder, and thus may have been graves. The plaza itself is thought to be a ritual space, wit a stone arrangement and earthen pits at the center.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
On the north side of the ring, dug-out pillared buildings are lined up in an orderly north–south direction. Eleven buildings with dug-out pillars were found in a row and 10 in a ring, but the pillar holes in three of the rows were extremely large, 80 cm in diameter, which is similar to those in eastern Japan. In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
A large amount of [[:en:Jōmon pottery|Jōmon pottery]] was excavated from the southeastern slope, totaling 179,237 pieces with a total weight of 2.8 tons. Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the [[:en:Stone tool|Stone tool]]s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of [[:en:maize weevil|maize weevil]]s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, [[:en:cockroach|cockroach]] egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from [[:en:Tano Station (Miyazaki)|Tano Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nippō Main Line|Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Motonobaru Site]][[ja:本野原遺跡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Motonobaru Site]][[ja:本野原遺跡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q85881084]] Wikidata name: Motonobaru Site [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Archaeological site in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Motonobaru Site | native_name =本野原遺跡 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = | map_type = Japan Miyazaki Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Motonobaru Site | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|31|49|23|N|131|16|57|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Miyazaki, Miyazaki|Miyazaki, Miyazaki]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Jōmon period]] | cultures = | discovered = 2000 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = None | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|49|23|N|131|16|57|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Motonobaru Site'''|本野原遺跡| Motonobaru iseki}} is an [[:en:Archaeological site|Archaeological site]] consisting of a complex settlement trace primarily from the [[Jōmon period]], located in the Tano neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Miyazaki (city)|Miyazaki]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]] on the of [[Kyushu]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164591|title=Database of National Cultural Properties |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref> More than 100 [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s have been excavated, and along with the [[:en:Yokomine Site|Yokomine Site]] on [[:en:Tanegashima|Tanegashima]], it is the largest ring settlement to have been found in western Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Overview== Motonobaru Site is located on a plateau at an elevation of approximately 180 meters on an [[:en:Alluvial fan|Alluvial fan]] at the foot of Mt. Wanitsukayama. In 2000, the Tano Town Board of Education conducted an [[archaeological excavation]] in conjunction with a prefectural farmland conservation and improvement project, and found that it was a complex of ruins of a large-scale village that began in the [[:en:Japanese Paleolithic|Japanese Paleolithic]] period and continued into the Jōmon period. This is extremely rare in the Kyushu region or indeed anywhere in western Japan west of [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]. The village was constructed in a ring shape of about 100 meters in diameter with earthen storage pits, dug pillar buildings, and [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s centered around a central plaza. The pit dwellings are concentrated on the north and east sides of the ring, and earthen pits are distributed on the outside adjacent to the plaza. There is considerable overlap on the foundations of these structures, indicating that they were rebuilt over centuries of occupation. Some of them were found to contain carbonized nuts, where as other contained bone powder, and thus may have been graves. The plaza itself is thought to be a ritual space, wit a stone arrangement and earthen pits at the center.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
On the north side of the ring, dug-out pillared buildings are lined up in an orderly north–south direction. Eleven buildings with dug-out pillars were found in a row and 10 in a ring, but the pillar holes in three of the rows were extremely large, 80 cm in diameter, which is similar to those in eastern Japan. In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
A large amount of [[:en:Jōmon pottery|Jōmon pottery]] was excavated from the southeastern slope, totaling 179,237 pieces with a total weight of 2.8 tons. Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the [[:en:Stone tool|Stone tool]]s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of [[:en:maize weevil|maize weevil]]s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, [[:en:cockroach|cockroach]] egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from [[:en:Tano Station (Miyazaki)|Tano Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nippō Main Line|Nippō Main Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.miyazaki-archive.jp/d-museum/mch/details/view/1838 Miyazaki Cultural Heritage Information] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.miyazaki.miyazaki.jp/faq/doc/1316.html Miyazaki city home page]{{in lang|ja}}

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Motonobaru Site]][[ja:本野原遺跡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Motonobaru Site]][[ja:本野原遺跡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q85881084]] Wikidata name: Motonobaru Site [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Archaeological site in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Motonobaru Site | native_name =本野原遺跡 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = | map_type = Japan Miyazaki Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Motonobaru Site | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|31|49|23|N|131|16|57|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Miyazaki, Miyazaki|Miyazaki, Miyazaki]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Jōmon period]] | cultures = | discovered = 2000 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = None | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|49|23|N|131|16|57|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Motonobaru Site'''|本野原遺跡| Motonobaru iseki}} is an {{ill|Archaeological site|en|Archaeological site|WD=Q839954|lt=Archaeological site}} consisting of a complex settlement trace primarily from the [[Jōmon period]], located in the Tano neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Miyazaki (city)|en|Miyazaki (city)|WD=Q204308|lt=Miyazaki}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}} on the of [[Kyushu]] {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164591|title=Database of National Cultural Properties |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref> More than 100 [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s have been excavated, and along with the {{ill|Yokomine Site|en|Yokomine Site|WD=Q11542680|lt=Yokomine Site}} on {{ill|Tanegashima|en|Tanegashima|WD=Q696228|lt=Tanegashima}}, it is the largest ring settlement to have been found in western Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Overview== Motonobaru Site is located on a plateau at an elevation of approximately 180 meters on an {{ill|Alluvial fan|en|Alluvial fan|WD=Q465088|lt=Alluvial fan}} at the foot of Mt. Wanitsukayama. In 2000, the Tano Town Board of Education conducted an [[archaeological excavation]] in conjunction with a prefectural farmland conservation and improvement project, and found that it was a complex of ruins of a large-scale village that began in the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|en|Japanese Paleolithic|WD=Q844172|lt=Japanese Paleolithic}} period and continued into the Jōmon period. This is extremely rare in the Kyushu region or indeed anywhere in western Japan west of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}. The village was constructed in a ring shape of about 100 meters in diameter with earthen storage pits, dug pillar buildings, and [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s centered around a central plaza. The pit dwellings are concentrated on the north and east sides of the ring, and earthen pits are distributed on the outside adjacent to the plaza. There is considerable overlap on the foundations of these structures, indicating that they were rebuilt over centuries of occupation. Some of them were found to contain carbonized nuts, where as other contained bone powder, and thus may have been graves. The plaza itself is thought to be a ritual space, wit a stone arrangement and earthen pits at the center.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
On the north side of the ring, dug-out pillared buildings are lined up in an orderly north–south direction. Eleven buildings with dug-out pillars were found in a row and 10 in a ring, but the pillar holes in three of the rows were extremely large, 80 cm in diameter, which is similar to those in eastern Japan. In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
A large amount of {{ill|Jōmon pottery|en|Jōmon pottery|WD=Q4390366|lt=Jōmon pottery}} was excavated from the southeastern slope, totaling 179,237 pieces with a total weight of 2.8 tons. Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|WD=Q479257|lt=Stone tool}}s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of {{ill|maize weevil|en|maize weevil|WD=Q3272137|lt=maize weevil}}s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, {{ill|cockroach|en|cockroach|WD=Q1661828|lt=cockroach}} egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from {{ill|Tano Station (Miyazaki)|en|Tano Station (Miyazaki)|WD=Q5357255|lt=Tano Station}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Nippō Main Line|en|Nippō Main Line|WD=Q1052288|lt=Nippō Main Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)|WD=Q6573277|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.miyazaki-archive.jp/d-museum/mch/details/view/1838 Miyazaki Cultural Heritage Information] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.miyazaki.miyazaki.jp/faq/doc/1316.html Miyazaki city home page]{{in lang|ja}}
{{Kofun navbar}}

Eleven buildings with dug-out pillars were found in a row and 10 in a ring, but the pillar holes in three of the rows were extremely large, 80 cm in diameter, which is similar to those in eastern Japan. In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
A large amount of {{ill|Jōmon pottery|en|Jōmon pottery|WD=Q4390366|lt=Jōmon pottery}} was excavated from the southeastern slope, totaling 179,237 pieces with a total weight of 2.8 tons. Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|WD=Q479257|lt=Stone tool}}s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of {{ill|maize weevil|en|maize weevil|WD=Q3272137|lt=maize weevil}}s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, {{ill|cockroach|en|cockroach|WD=Q1661828|lt=cockroach}} egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from {{ill|Tano Station (Miyazaki)|en|Tano Station (Miyazaki)|WD=Q5357255|lt=Tano Station}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Nippō Main Line|en|Nippō Main Line|WD=Q1052288|lt=Nippō Main Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Motonobaru Site'''|本野原遺跡| Motonobaru iseki}} is an {{ill|Archaeological site|en|Archaeological site|WD=Q839954|lt=Archaeological site}} consisting of a complex settlement trace primarily from the [[Jōmon period]], located in the Tano neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Miyazaki (city)|en|Miyazaki (city)|WD=Q204308|lt=Miyazaki}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}} on the of [[Kyushu]] {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164591|title=Database of National Cultural Properties |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref> More than 100 [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s have been excavated, and along with the {{ill|Yokomine Site|en|Yokomine Site|WD=Q11542680|lt=Yokomine Site}} on {{ill|Tanegashima|en|Tanegashima|WD=Q696228|lt=Tanegashima}}, it is the largest ring settlement to have been found in western Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Overview== Motonobaru Site is located on a plateau at an elevation of approximately 180 meters on an {{ill|Alluvial fan|en|Alluvial fan|WD=Q465088|lt=Alluvial fan}} at the foot of Mt. Wanitsukayama. In 2000, the Tano Town Board of Education conducted an [[archaeological excavation]] in conjunction with a prefectural farmland conservation and improvement project, and found that it was a complex of ruins of a large-scale village that began in the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|en|Japanese Paleolithic|WD=Q844172|lt=Japanese Paleolithic}} period and continued into the Jōmon period. This is extremely rare in the Kyushu region or indeed anywhere in western Japan west of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}. The village was constructed in a ring shape of about 100 meters in diameter with earthen storage pits, dug pillar buildings, and [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s centered around a central plaza. The pit dwellings are concentrated on the north and east sides of the ring, and earthen pits are distributed on the outside adjacent to the plaza. There is considerable overlap on the foundations of these structures, indicating that they were rebuilt over centuries of occupation. Some of them were found to contain carbonized nuts, where as other contained bone powder, and thus may have been graves. The plaza itself is thought to be a ritual space, wit a stone arrangement and earthen pits at the center.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
On the north side of the ring, dug-out pillared buildings are lined up in an orderly north–south direction. Eleven buildings with dug-out pillars were found in a row and 10 in a ring, but the pillar holes in three of the rows were extremely large, 80 cm in diameter, which is similar to those in eastern Japan. In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|WD=Q479257|lt=Stone tool}}s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of {{ill|maize weevil|en|maize weevil|WD=Q3272137|lt=maize weevil}}s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, {{ill|cockroach|en|cockroach|WD=Q1661828|lt=cockroach}} egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from {{ill|Tano Station (Miyazaki)|en|Tano Station (Miyazaki)|WD=Q5357255|lt=Tano Station}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Nippō Main Line|en|Nippō Main Line|WD=Q1052288|lt=Nippō Main Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Motonobaru Site'''|本野原遺跡| Motonobaru iseki}} is an {{ill|Archaeological site|en|Archaeological site|WD=Q839954|lt=Archaeological site}} consisting of a complex settlement trace primarily from the [[Jōmon period]], located in the Tano neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Miyazaki (city)|en|Miyazaki (city)|WD=Q204308|lt=Miyazaki}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}} on the of [[Kyushu]] {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164591|title=Database of National Cultural Properties |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref> More than 100 [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s have been excavated, and along with the {{ill|Yokomine Site|en|Yokomine Site|WD=Q11542680|lt=Yokomine Site}} on {{ill|Tanegashima|en|Tanegashima|WD=Q696228|lt=Tanegashima}}, it is the largest ring settlement to have been found in western Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Overview== Motonobaru Site is located on a plateau at an elevation of approximately 180 meters on an {{ill|Alluvial fan|en|Alluvial fan|WD=Q465088|lt=Alluvial fan}} at the foot of Mt. Wanitsukayama. In 2000, the Tano Town Board of Education conducted an [[archaeological excavation]] in conjunction with a prefectural farmland conservation and improvement project, and found that it was a complex of ruins of a large-scale village that began in the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|en|Japanese Paleolithic|WD=Q844172|lt=Japanese Paleolithic}} period and continued into the Jōmon period. This is extremely rare in the Kyushu region or indeed anywhere in western Japan west of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}. The village was constructed in a ring shape of about 100 meters in diameter with earthen storage pits, dug pillar buildings, and [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s centered around a central plaza. The pit dwellings are concentrated on the north and east sides of the ring, and earthen pits are distributed on the outside adjacent to the plaza. There is considerable overlap on the foundations of these structures, indicating that they were rebuilt over centuries of occupation. Some of them were found to contain carbonized nuts, where as other contained bone powder, and thus may have been graves. The plaza itself is thought to be a ritual space, wit a stone arrangement and earthen pits at the center.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
On the north side of the ring, dug-out pillared buildings are lined up in an orderly north–south direction. Eleven buildings with dug-out pillars were found in a row and 10 in a ring, but the pillar holes in three of the rows were extremely large, 80 cm in diameter, which is similar to those in eastern Japan. In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
A large amount of {{ill|Jōmon pottery|en|Jōmon pottery|WD=Q4390366|lt=Jōmon pottery}} was excavated from the southeastern slope, totaling 179,237 pieces with a total weight of 2.8 tons. Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|WD=Q479257|lt=Stone tool}}s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of {{ill|maize weevil|en|maize weevil|WD=Q3272137|lt=maize weevil}}s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, {{ill|cockroach|en|cockroach|WD=Q1661828|lt=cockroach}} egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from {{ill|Tano Station (Miyazaki)|en|Tano Station (Miyazaki)|WD=Q5357255|lt=Tano Station}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Nippō Main Line|en|Nippō Main Line|WD=Q1052288|lt=Nippō Main Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)|WD=Q6573277|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://www.miyazaki-archive.jp/d-museum/mch/details/view/1838 Miyazaki Cultural Heritage Information] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.miyazaki.miyazaki.jp/faq/doc/1316.html Miyazaki city home page]{{in lang|ja}}

In addition, 173 impressions of {{ill|maize weevil|en|maize weevil|WD=Q3272137|lt=maize weevil|1=Maize weevil}}s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, {{ill|cockroach|en|cockroach|WD=Q1661828|lt=cockroach|1=Cockroach}} egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from {{ill|Tano Station (Miyazaki)|en|Tano Station (Miyazaki)|WD=Q5357255|lt=Tano Station|1=Tano Station (Miyazaki)}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Nippō Main Line|en|Nippō Main Line|WD=Q1052288|lt=Nippō Main Line|1=Nippō Main Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)|WD=Q6573277|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)}}

{{Short description|Archaeological site in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Motonobaru Site | native_name =本野原遺跡 | native_name_lang = | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = | map_type = Japan Miyazaki Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Motonobaru Site | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|31|49|23|N|131|16|57|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Miyazaki, Miyazaki|Miyazaki, Miyazaki]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = settlement | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Jōmon period]] | cultures = | discovered = 2000 | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = None | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|49|23|N|131|16|57|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Motonobaru Site'''|本野原遺跡| Motonobaru iseki}} is an {{ill|Archaeological site|en|Archaeological site|WD=Q839954|lt=Archaeological site|1=Archaeological site}} consisting of a complex settlement trace primarily from the [[Jōmon period]], located in the Tano neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Miyazaki (city)|en|Miyazaki (city)|WD=Q204308|lt=Miyazaki|1=Miyazaki (city)}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}} on the of [[Kyushu]] {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/164591|title=Database of National Cultural Properties |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2023}}</ref> More than 100 [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s have been excavated, and along with the {{ill|Yokomine Site|en|Yokomine Site|WD=Q11542680|lt=Yokomine Site|1=Yokomine Site}} on {{ill|Tanegashima|en|Tanegashima|WD=Q696228|lt=Tanegashima|1=Tanegashima}}, it is the largest ring settlement to have been found in western Japan.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Overview== Motonobaru Site is located on a plateau at an elevation of approximately 180 meters on an {{ill|Alluvial fan|en|Alluvial fan|WD=Q465088|lt=Alluvial fan|1=Alluvial fan}} at the foot of Mt. Wanitsukayama. In 2000, the Tano Town Board of Education conducted an [[archaeological excavation]] in conjunction with a prefectural farmland conservation and improvement project, and found that it was a complex of ruins of a large-scale village that began in the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|en|Japanese Paleolithic|WD=Q844172|lt=Japanese Paleolithic|1=Japanese Paleolithic}} period and continued into the Jōmon period. This is extremely rare in the Kyushu region or indeed anywhere in western Japan west of {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. The village was constructed in a ring shape of about 100 meters in diameter with earthen storage pits, dug pillar buildings, and [[:en:Pit dwelling|Pit dwelling]]s centered around a central plaza. The pit dwellings are concentrated on the north and east sides of the ring, and earthen pits are distributed on the outside adjacent to the plaza. There is considerable overlap on the foundations of these structures, indicating that they were rebuilt over centuries of occupation. Some of them were found to contain carbonized nuts, where as other contained bone powder, and thus may have been graves. The plaza itself is thought to be a ritual space, wit a stone arrangement and earthen pits at the center.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
On the north side of the ring, dug-out pillared buildings are lined up in an orderly north–south direction. Eleven buildings with dug-out pillars were found in a row and 10 in a ring, but the pillar holes in three of the rows were extremely large, 80 cm in diameter, which is similar to those in eastern Japan. In addition, a road with a hardened surface has been confirmed in the northwestern part of the village.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
A large amount of {{ill|Jōmon pottery|en|Jōmon pottery|WD=Q4390366|lt=Jōmon pottery|1=Jōmon pottery}} was excavated from the southeastern slope, totaling 179,237 pieces with a total weight of 2.8 tons. Most of the excavated artifacts are earthenware, and a high proportion of the {{ill|Stone tool|en|Stone tool|WD=Q479257|lt=Stone tool|1=Stone tool}}s are food processing tools such as grinding stones and stone plates. It is speculated that the village moved westward, based on the fact that ruins and excavated artifacts are most densely distributed on the west side of the village. In addition, 173 impressions of {{ill|maize weevil|en|maize weevil|WD=Q3272137|lt=maize weevil|1=Maize weevil}}s were found, accounting for more than half of the total number of maize weevil excavated during the Jōmon period nationwide. In 2016, {{ill|cockroach|en|cockroach|WD=Q1661828|lt=cockroach|1=Cockroach}} egg sacs were discovered in the impressions of Jōmon pottery excavated at this site, and was reported to be the oldest cockroach in Japan. This species cockroach is native to southern China, and it is thought that it may have come to this site by boat.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is approximately 18 minutes by car from {{ill|Tano Station (Miyazaki)|en|Tano Station (Miyazaki)|WD=Q5357255|lt=Tano Station|1=Tano Station (Miyazaki)}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Nippō Main Line|en|Nippō Main Line|WD=Q1052288|lt=Nippō Main Line|1=Nippō Main Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)|WD=Q6573277|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Miyazaki)}}
